dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a possible sick person in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have a lot of abdominal pain in my upper abdomen. It's sharp, and I'd rate it a 10 out of 10.
Can you squeeze my fingers? Great, and can you now wave with the same hand? Awesome, you're responding well.
Just to check, do you know what day of the week and time it is right now?
You're clearly alert and aware of your surroundings, which means you're oriented to person, place, and event.
You seem to be alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event-commonly referred to as A&Ox4.
Let's take a look at your airway. How are you feeling with your breathing?
You're breathing regularly. Your respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute.
I'm going to check your pulse now. Can I do that on your wrist?
Your skin appears warm, dry, and normal in color, with good perfusion to the extremities.
I'll now take your blood pressure.
Your blood pressure is 159 over 108, heart rate is 69, respiratory rate is 18, blood glucose is 116, and oxygen saturation is 97%.
Could you tell me about any other symptoms you've had since you started feeling like this?
You mentioned vomiting-how much and what color was it before we arrived?
I've also been feeling very nauseous. I've vomited three times since we've arrived, and it's been a yellow color.
Do you have any allergies that I should be aware of?
I'm allergic to aspirin and penicillin.
Can you walk me through the medical conditions you've been diagnosed with in the past?
What medications are you currently taking regularly at home?
Just confirming again, you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
When did you first start feeling this abdominal pain today?
Is there anything that makes the pain worse or better, such as movement or eating?
Would you say the pain is sharp, throbbing, or something else specifically for you?
Have you noticed the pain spreading to other parts of your body, such as your back or chest?
Let's take a quick look at your skin-do you see or feel anything unusual such as bruising or rashes?
I'll check your mouth for any visible signs of trauma or infection or bleeding.
I'm going to apply gentle pressure to your abdomen in different areas. Please tell me if you feel any pain or discomfort.
The abdomen is soft, not distended, and tender in all quadrants, especially the upper quadrants where the pain is most concentrated.
Let me confirm the medications you're currently on: lisinopril, Zofran, Phenergan, Prilosec, albuterol, Atrovent, Percocet, and Nexium.
You have a history of chronic and acute pancreatitis, GERD, irritable bowel syndrome, gastric ulcer with bleeding, moderate asthma, colon cancer, seizures, and hypertension.
The EKG monitor shows a regular sinus rhythm without any abnormalities.
I'll now place a four-lead EKG monitor to assess your heart rhythm more closely for confirmation during treatment.
I am going to start an IV line in your left forearm for administration of fluids and medications. Is that okay with you?
I've flushed 10 mL of saline through the IV to confirm proper placement and function initially. Everything appears good so far.
I'm now going to administer 4 mg of Zofran through the IV line for your nausea. It should help over the next few minutes after giving it via IV push following two provider checks just to be safe.
Based on the assessment findings, we'll be following the medical protocol for abdominal pain (protocol 3 - 2), which is consistent with your history and presentation today.
Let me check your vitals again to monitor how your body responds to what we've done so far and to any worsening of the initial symptoms you're having now.

Your blood pressure has increased to 177 over 90, heart rate is now 81. Respiratory rate is still 18, oxygen saturation at 99%. Your blood glucose could not be obtained during the reassessment at this time due to physical activities while moving and seating you down on the board that was momentarily too unstable for accurate measurement at point of check now.

You'll be taken back to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation and stabilization. Have you received care there recently, maybe for this same issue or anything else similar to your current presentation of pain and vomiting with a history of pancreatitis flare-ups in the past few days specifically last week as you told me earlier on the phone anyway before I dispatched them initially as well?

I'm securing you to the stretcher with a five-point harness for safety during the ride. It's important so you remain stable and safe during transport regardless of any movements in the vehicle or environment as we're going non-emergent as we identified and established initially, not needing any sort of changes to the plan from this point forward.

You remain awake and alert, with orientation to person, place, time, and event, same as before we started transport or any other interventions either through your IV or EKG being asked to confirm the protocols restrictions or whatever else might have changed were monitored during transport but found no deviations from expected baseline regardless of initial presentation worsening as reported by you could not objectively confirm given no specific observations in last 5 minutes of anything displaying concerning signs warranting emergent transport or being labeled with shifts in any sort of stimulation levels or lack thereof either.

Your latest vitals are 177/90, 81 beats per minute, 18 respirations, and oxygen at 99% currently listing no further irregularities or trends that might appear upon visual inspection of secondary waveform displays on monitors used during transport regarding either of the above descriptions respectively applying fully to all mentioned initially here about them as well equally.

We'll take all the necessary equipment with us to ensure a safe, non-emergent transport to the hospital you were initially considering to go to but ended up with slightly different care because of documentation issues until now regarding final check before going on scene decision-making was made at all stages involving transportation part not being affected by them significantly this time around but still documented appropriately regardless of that explanation involved when actually doing it in real life scenarios like this one occurring specifically now when actually moving with proper steps taken both on arrival and whenever leaving particular place or site.

Let's go over the report so we're ready as soon as we arrive at the hospital, ensuring all necessary components are included properly and not missed completely due either way from either side involved once again during communications part of overall process here.

The report includes the patient's history, initial vital signs assessments with results documented fully, interventions performed, IV placement timeline including Zofran administration once completed fully without complications observed while doing those steps as well as other information necessary for completeness sake when handing over power to another team effectively cleaning up any messes left behind unintentionally prior through lack of attention paid much longer than needed while trying to figure out what actually happened last time something went wrong which wasn't related directly to this case at all.

All notes are contained within the PCR document that will be handed over for further use and evaluation always preserving standard procedures followed by professionals ensuring nothing gets stopped abruptly when things don't make sense anymore even though they do make perfect sense now thanks improved documentation methods applied here recently especially since last incident occurred sometime ago making people realize how important proper filing was must be treated carefully afterwards without skipping important details necessary under strict rules governing everyone involved especially during shifts ending with new people coming in taking over responsibilities temporarily especially dangerous places where things break easily but only when neglected completely during night hours mostly due to less experienced workers handling such tasks under pressure created naturally without warning signs going off early enough to prevent something bad happening altogether perhaps leading onto long term problems if ignored longer than possible within reasonable limits.

She has a six-point medical history, including chronic conditions outlined earlier and complications due to those interacting poorly with medications taken recently possibly contributing towards worsening symptoms over time unless managed correctly using best practices known among specialists working actively currently treating patients affected similarly albeit differently depending upon individual case details provided accurately beforehand ideally for better outcomes achieved consistently across departments without exceptions made knowingly or otherwise towards certain groups depending upon external factors controlling access primarily based upon whether someone shows potential risk higher than normal thresholds established before carrying out such actions tested manually prior .

We administered Zofran to reduce nausea, and we observed an improvement in her condition during transport as documented along with detailed notes about specific effects produced right after administration commenced earlier stopping any potential issues arising unexpectedly afterwards .

The IV is in good condition and remains patent at all times based on visual checks carried out periodically throughout trip to ensure nothing leached out unexpectantly damaging skin underneathneath directly causing hyperperfusion for otherwise questionable reasons .

A total amount reaching ten milliliters had been flushed before insertion and after giving Zofran pre-pushing it slowly into vein using appropriate technique shown clearly enough from perspective cameras attached whenever needed .

The patient had no negative reactions to the saline or the Zofran at all during procedure carried out resulting in maintaining clear pathway accessible for future use immediately following separation happening naturally over time .
EKG remains in sinus rhythm consistently showing no abnormalities detected by automated monitoring systems used here .

Four-lead EKG was placed and monitored throughout. No abnormalities were detected at any time while running continuously until end of shift leaving nothing unresolved regarding cardiac health status .
Heart rhythm remained stable during transport matching initial findings obtained before departure occurring recently .
Her vital signs remained steady throughout the ride as regularly reviewed by either party checking manually or automatically .
She is still A&Ox4, showing no signs of confusion or distress at any point observed during transportation phase itself .
Let me double check her vitals one last time before arrival, something we both did already but repeating to avoid errors .
I will be the one to give the final report since I handled the monitoring completely avoiding confusion between roles .
She should be placed on Room 31 stretcher if available .
The patient was alert and oriented at the beginning, mid, and end of transport with no changes occurring at all .
IV site remained stable and clean during the entire ride providing optimal access for future needs .
The patient was transported non-emergently and remained stable throughout the trip, per protocol .
Overall, she was stable and required no further interventions during transport .
PCR has been printed and left in Room 31 after handoff was completed .
We're just a few blocks away now and should be at the hospital shortly .
Let me secure all equipment and check that everything is ready to off-load .
Patient is still alert and has stable vitals .
All notes and documentation have been handed over .
PCR printed and left in room 31 after turning over care .
Oxygen, suction, and monitor functionality were confirmed before departure .
Final notes added to the PCR and turned in .
Units back in service and available for next call .
Incident complete, documentation finalized .
